title: Correspondence Theory — Sahlqvist Formulas and the van Benthem Algorithm

sections.title: 11.1 Applications of Modal Logic and the Need for Frame Classes

questions.text: Which of the following are correct interpretations of $\Box p$ in different modal logics? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In epistemic logic: $\Box p$ means 'I know $p$ to be true'
In deontic logic: $\Box p$ means '$p$ is obligatory'
In temporal logic: $\Box p$ means '$p$ will be true'
In doxastic logic: $\Box p$ means '$p$ is possible'
In dynamic logic: $\Box p$ means 'after action $\alpha$, $p$ holds'

explanations.text: Epistemic logic interprets $\Box$ as knowledge.
explanations.notes_quote: 'I know $p$ to be true' | Epistemic logic | Knowledge

explanations.text: Deontic logic interprets $\Box$ as obligation.
explanations.notes_quote: '$p$ is obligatory' | Deontic logic | Obligation/permission

explanations.text: Temporal logic interprets $\Box$ as future truth.
explanations.notes_quote: '$p$ will be true' | Temporal logic | Time

explanations.text: In doxastic logic, $\Box p$ means 'the agent believes $p$', not '$p$ is possible'. Possibility is expressed by $\Diamond p$.
Dynamic logic uses $\Box$ for post-condition of actions.
explanations.notes_quote: 'After action $\alpha$, $p$ holds' | Dynamic logic | Programs/transitions

questions.text: Which of the following are correct statements about the epistemic axioms for knowledge? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: True Knowledge: $\Box p \to p$ — if I know $p$, then $p$ must be true
Positive Introspection: $\Box p \to \Box\Box p$ — if I know $p$, I know that I know $p$
Negative Introspection: $\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$ — if I don't know $p$, I know that I don't know $p$
All three epistemic axioms are valid over the class of all Kripke frames
True Knowledge $\Box p \to p$ is valid precisely over reflexive frames

explanations.text: True Knowledge requires the agent cannot know false things.
explanations.notes_quote: True Knowledge: $\Box p \to p$ — 'If I know $p$, then $p$ must be true (I can't know false things)'

explanations.text: Positive Introspection is the axiom that knowledge of $p$ implies awareness of that knowledge.
explanations.notes_quote: Positive Introspection: $\Box p \to \Box\Box p$ — 'If I know $p$, I know that I know $p$'

explanations.text: Negative Introspection states that ignorance is known.
explanations.notes_quote: Negative Introspection: $\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$ — 'If I don't know $p$, I know that I don't know $p$'

explanations.text: None of these three axioms is valid over the class of ALL Kripke frames — they require specific frame properties (reflexivity, transitivity, Euclideanness).
explanations.notes_quote: None of these is valid over the class of all Kripke frames.

explanations.text: True Knowledge corresponds exactly to reflexive frames.
explanations.notes_quote: True Knowledge $\Box p \to p$ is valid over reflexive frames.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly capture Rumsfeld's epistemological categories in modal logic? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Known knowns: $\Box p$
Known unknowns: $\Box\neg\Box p$

Unknown unknowns: $
eg\Box p \wedge 
eg\Box
eg\Box p$
Unknown unknowns are consistent with Negative Introspection
Unknown unknowns directly refute Negative Introspection

explanations.text: Known knowns are things the agent knows, expressed as $\Box p$.
explanations.notes_quote: Known knowns: $\Box p$ (I know $p$)

explanations.text: Known unknowns are things the agent knows they don't know.
explanations.notes_quote: Known unknowns: $\Box\neg\Box p$ (I know that I don't know $p$)

explanations.text: Unknown unknowns are things the agent neither knows nor knows that they don't know.
explanations.notes_quote:
Unknown unknowns: $
eg\Box p \wedge 
eg\Box
eg\Box p$ (I neither know $p$ nor know that I don't know $p$)

explanations.text: Unknown unknowns REFUTE Negative Introspection — they show a scenario where an agent does not know $p$ yet also does not know that they don't know $p$.
Negative Introspection requires that not knowing $p$ implies knowing you don't know $p$ — unknown unknowns violate this.
explanations.notes_quote:
Unknown unknowns ($
eg\Box p \wedge 
eg\Box
eg\Box p$) directly refute Negative Introspection.

questions.source_ref: Section 11.2.1: Translating Kripke Models to Predicate Logic
questions.text: When translating a Kripke model $\mathfrak{M} = \langle W, R, V \rangle$ to a first-order predicate model $\mathfrak{M}^*$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Each propositional variable $p_i$ corresponds to a unary predicate symbol $P_i$
The accessibility relation $R$ corresponds to a binary predicate symbol $R$
$P_i^\mathcal{I} = V(p_i)$ for all propositional variables
Each propositional variable $p_i$ corresponds to a binary predicate symbol
The semantics of $\Diamond$ resembles an existential quantifier over accessible worlds

explanations.text: Propositional variables become unary predicates in the first-order translation.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2.1: Translating Kripke Models to Predicate Logic
explanations.notes_quote: A unary predicate symbol $P_i \in \text{Pred}$ for each propositional variable $p_i \in \text{Prop}$

explanations.text: The accessibility relation becomes a binary predicate in the first-order model.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2.1: Translating Kripke Models to Predicate Logic
explanations.notes_quote: A binary predicate symbol $R \in \text{Pred}$ for the accessibility relation

explanations.text: The interpretation of $P_i$ in the first-order model is exactly the valuation of $p_i$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2.1: Translating Kripke Models to Predicate Logic
explanations.notes_quote: $P_i^\mathcal{I} = V(p_i)$ for all $p_i \in \text{Prop}$

explanations.text: Propositional variables become UNARY predicates (applied to the current world variable), not binary predicates.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2.1: Translating Kripke Models to Predicate Logic

explanations.text: The $\Diamond$ operator is semantically analogous to existential quantification over accessible worlds.
explanations.section_ref: Section 11.2.1: Translating Kripke Models to Predicate Logic
explanations.notes_quote: The semantics of $\Diamond$ and $\Box$ is reminiscent of first-order quantifiers: $\mathfrak{M}, w \models \Diamond F \iff \exists u \in W;\ (w,u)\in R \text{ and } \mathfrak{M}, u \models F$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the standard translation $\text{ST}_x$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\text{ST}_x(p_i) = P_i(x)$
$\text{ST}_x(\Diamond G) = \exists y\bigl(R(x,y) \wedge \text{ST}_y(G)\bigr)$ where $y$ is a fresh variable
$\text{ST}_x(\Box G) = \forall y\bigl(R(x,y) \to \text{ST}_y(G)\bigr)$ where $y$ is a fresh variable
$\text{ST}_x(\Diamond G) = \forall y\bigl(R(x,y) \to \text{ST}_y(G)\bigr)$
$\text{ST}_x(G \wedge H) = \text{ST}_x(G) \wedge \text{ST}_x(H)$

explanations.text: Propositional variables translate to the corresponding unary predicate applied at the current world $x$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{ST}_x(p_i) = P_i(x)$

explanations.text: $\Diamond$ translates to existential quantification over an accessible world $y$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{ST}_x(\Diamond G) = \exists y\bigl(R(x,y) \wedge \text{ST}_y(G)\bigr)$

explanations.text: $\Box$ translates to universal quantification over accessible worlds.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{ST}_x(\Box G) = \forall y\bigl(R(x,y) \to \text{ST}_y(G)\bigr)$

explanations.text: This is the translation for $\Box$, not $\Diamond$. $\Diamond G$ uses an existential quantifier with $\wedge$, not a universal with $\to$.
Boolean connectives translate homomorphically — the world variable $x$ is passed to both sides unchanged.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{ST}_x(G \odot H) = \text{ST}_x(G) \odot \text{ST}_x(H)$ for $\odot \in \{\wedge, \vee, \to\}$

questions.text: Compute $\text{ST}_x(\Box p \to p)$. Which of the following is correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\text{ST}_x(\Box p \to p) = \forall y(R(x,y) \to P(y)) \to P(x)$
$\text{ST}_x(\Box p \to p) = \exists y(R(x,y) \wedge P(y)) \to P(x)$
This is the standard translation of the True Knowledge axiom (Axiom T)
This formula is in purely first-order logic (no predicate quantifiers)
$\text{ST}_x(\Box p \to p) = \forall y(R(x,y) \to P(y)) \wedge P(x)$

explanations.text: $\text{ST}_x(\Box p) = \forall y(R(x,y) \to P(y))$ and $\text{ST}_x(p) = P(x)$, so $\text{ST}_x(\Box p \to p) = \forall y(R(x,y)\to P(y)) \to P(x)$.
explanations.notes_quote: SGT 11.1(i): $\text{ST}_x(F) = \forall y(R(x,y) \to P(y)) \to P(x)$

explanations.text: Using $\exists$ with $\wedge$ would be the translation of $\Diamond p \to p$, not $\Box p \to p$. $\Box$ translates to $\forall$ with $\to$.
$\Box p \to p$ is the True Knowledge (Reflexivity) axiom, Axiom T in the table of standard axioms.
explanations.notes_quote: T (Reflexivity): $\Box p \to p$ | Reflexive | $\forall x\,R(x,x)$

explanations.text: The standard translation itself is a first-order formula with free predicate symbols $P$ — it is a first-order formula (not second-order), with $P$ as a free predicate.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{ST}_x(F)$ is defined inductively... all resulting formulas are first-order

explanations.text: Using $\wedge$ instead of $\to$ between the antecedent and consequent is incorrect — the implication $\to$ must be preserved.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state Theorem 11.2 and its implications? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\mathfrak{M}, x \models F \iff \mathfrak{M}^* \models^h \text{ST}_x(F)$ where $h(x) = x$
Frame validity of $F$ requires quantifying over predicates $P_1, \ldots, P_n$, which takes us into second-order logic
Quantifying over predicates is required because propositional variables represent arbitrary sets of worlds
Frame validity can always be expressed as a first-order condition without any further work
Global satisfiability of $F$ in $\mathfrak{M}$ corresponds to $\forall x\,\text{ST}_x(F)$ being satisfiable in $\mathfrak{M}^*$

explanations.text: This is the exact statement of Theorem 11.2.
explanations.notes_quote: $\mathfrak{M}, x \models F \iff \mathfrak{M}^* \models^h \text{ST}_x(F)$

explanations.text: Frame validity requires the formula to hold for ALL valuations, which means quantifying over all predicates $P_i$ — second-order logic.
explanations.notes_quote: $\forall P_1 \ldots \forall P_n\,\forall x\,\text{ST}_x(F)$ is not a well-formed formula of first-order predicate logic — we are quantifying over predicates, not individual variables. This takes us into second-order predicate logic.

explanations.text: Propositional variables can be satisfied by any subset of worlds, so all possible valuations must be considered for frame validity.
explanations.notes_quote: To obtain a first-order condition on the frame, we must eliminate the predicate quantifiers $P_1, \ldots, P_n$

explanations.text: Frame validity cannot ALWAYS be expressed as a first-order condition — Chagrova's Theorem shows this is undecidable in general.
Global satisfiability (truth at all worlds) corresponds to universal quantification over the world variable in the first-order model.
explanations.notes_quote: Global satisfiability: $F$ is globally satisfied in $\mathfrak{M}$ iff $\forall x\,\text{ST}_x(F)$ is satisfiable in $\mathfrak{M}^*$

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe Chagrova's Theorem? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: It is undecidable whether a given modal axiom has a first-order correspondent
It implies there is no general algorithm that, given any modal formula, produces its first-order frame correspondent
It proves that NO modal formula has a first-order correspondent
Despite Chagrova's Theorem, large useful subclasses of formulas do have computable correspondents
Chagrova's Theorem is about the undecidability of modal satisfiability

explanations.text: This is the exact statement of Chagrova's Theorem.
explanations.notes_quote: Theorem 11.3 (Chagrova's Theorem): It is undecidable whether a given modal axiom has a first-order correspondent.

explanations.text: Undecidability means no algorithm can determine this in general.
explanations.notes_quote: This means there is no general algorithm that, given any modal formula, produces its first-order frame correspondent.

explanations.text: Chagrova's Theorem says we cannot DECIDE whether a correspondent exists in general — many formulas (like all Sahlqvist formulas) DO have first-order correspondents.
Sahlqvist formulas are a large useful subclass with computable correspondents, despite the general undecidability.
explanations.notes_quote: However, large useful subclasses of formulas do have computable correspondents.

explanations.text: Chagrova's Theorem is about the undecidability of FRAME CORRESPONDENCE, not modal satisfiability. Modal satisfiability (for basic modal logic K) is decidable.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe positive and negative formulas? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $F$ is positive w.r.t. $p$ if every occurrence of $p$ appears under an even number of negations (in CNF)
$F$ is negative w.r.t. $p$ if every occurrence of $p$ appears under an odd number of negations (in CNF)
Positive formulas are monotonically increasing in the valuation: if $V(p) \subseteq V'(p)$ and $\mathfrak{M}, w \models F$, then $\mathfrak{M}', w \models F$
Negative formulas are monotonically increasing in the valuation
A formula $F$ is positive if it is positive w.r.t. ALL propositional variables in $F$

explanations.text: Even number of negations means the variable appears positively.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is positive (resp. negative) with respect to a variable $p \in \text{Prop}$ if every occurrence of $p$ in the conjunctive normal form of $F$ appears under the scope of an even (resp. odd) number of negation symbols.

explanations.text: Odd number of negations means the variable appears negatively.
explanations.notes_quote: every occurrence of $p$ in the conjunctive normal form of $F$ appears under the scope of an even (resp. odd) number of negation symbols

explanations.text: This is Lemma 11.5(i) — the monotonicity property of positive formulas.
explanations.notes_quote: Lemma 11.5 (Monotonicity). If $F$ is positive: $\mathfrak{M}, w \models F \Rightarrow \mathfrak{M}', w \models F$ for all $w \in W$ [when $V(p) \subseteq V'(p)$]

explanations.text: Negative formulas are monotonically DECREASING, not increasing. Making more things true can falsify a negative formula.
explanations.notes_quote: Positive formulas are monotonically increasing in the valuation; negative formulas are monotonically decreasing.

explanations.text: A formula is globally positive if it is positive with respect to all of its propositional variables.
explanations.notes_quote: $F$ is positive (resp. negative) if it is positive (resp. negative) with respect to all propositional variables appearing in $F$.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly define atoms and boxed atoms? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An atom is any propositional variable $p \in \text{Prop}$
A boxed atom is any formula of the form $\Box^n p$ for some $p \in \text{Prop}$ and $n \geq 1$
$\Box p$, $\Box\Box q$, and $\Box\Box\Box r$ are all examples of boxed atoms
A boxed atom requires $n \geq 0$, so plain propositional variables also count as boxed atoms
$\Diamond p$ is a boxed atom

explanations.text: Atoms are simply propositional variables.
explanations.notes_quote: An atom is any propositional variable $p \in \text{Prop}$

explanations.text: Boxed atoms require one or more $\Box$ operators applied to a propositional variable.
explanations.notes_quote: A boxed atom is any formula of the form $\Box^n p$ for some $p \in \text{Prop}$ and $n \geq 1$ — i.e., a propositional variable under one or more $\Box$ operators

explanations.text: These are explicitly given as examples of boxed atoms in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Examples of boxed atoms: $\Box p$, $\Box\Box q$, $\Box\Box\Box r$

explanations.text: Boxed atoms require $n \geq 1$ — plain propositional variables (with $n=0$) are atoms, not boxed atoms.
$\Diamond p$ is a diamond formula applied to an atom, not a boxed atom. Boxed atoms require only $\Box$ operators.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly define Sahlqvist antecedents and Sahlqvist implications? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A Sahlqvist antecedent is built from $\top$, $\bot$, atoms, boxed atoms, and negative formulas using $\wedge$ and $\vee$ only
A Sahlqvist implication is $F \to G$ where $F$ is a Sahlqvist antecedent and $G$ is a positive formula
A Sahlqvist formula can be built from Sahlqvist implications using $\Box$, and $\wedge$/$\vee$ between variable-disjoint parts
A Sahlqvist antecedent can freely use $\Diamond$ applied to $\Box$ formulas (e.g., $\Diamond\Box p$ is allowed)
A Sahlqvist antecedent can freely use $\Box$ applied to $\Diamond$ formulas (e.g., $\Box\Diamond p$ is allowed)

explanations.text: This is the definition of a Sahlqvist antecedent.
explanations.notes_quote: A Sahlqvist antecedent is a modal formula built from $\top$, $\bot$, atoms, boxed atoms, and negative formulas using only $\wedge$ (conjunction) and $\vee$ (disjunction).

explanations.text: A Sahlqvist implication requires a Sahlqvist antecedent and a positive consequent.
explanations.notes_quote: A Sahlqvist implication is a modal formula of the form $F \to G$ where: $F$ is a Sahlqvist antecedent, and $G$ is a positive formula

explanations.text: This is the definition of a Sahlqvist formula built from implications.
explanations.notes_quote: A Sahlqvist formula is a formula built from Sahlqvist implications by: freely applying $\Box$, and applying $\wedge$ and $\vee$ only between formulas that share no propositional variables

explanations.text: Sahlqvist antecedents allow nesting $\Box$ inside $\Diamond$ — e.g., $\Diamond\Box p$ is a valid Sahlqvist antecedent component.
explanations.notes_quote: Sahlqvist antecedents allow nesting of $\Box$ inside $\Diamond$ (e.g., $\Diamond\Box p$)

explanations.text: Sahlqvist antecedents do NOT allow $\Diamond$ inside $\Box$ — e.g., $\Box\Diamond p$ is NOT a Sahlqvist antecedent because $\Diamond p$ is not negative.
explanations.notes_quote: but NOT $\Diamond$ inside $\Box$ (e.g., $\Box\Diamond p$ is not a Sahlqvist antecedent because $\Diamond p$ is not negative)

questions.text: Which of the following formulas are Sahlqvist formulas (or Sahlqvist implications)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $p \to \Diamond p$ (antecedent: atom $p$; consequent: $\Diamond p$ is positive)
$(p \wedge \Diamond\Box p) \to \Box p$ (antecedent: atom and boxed atom under $\Diamond$; consequent: positive)
$\Diamond\Box p \to \Box\Diamond p$ (consequent $\Box\Diamond p$ is positive)
$\Box p \to p$
$\Box p \to \Box\Box p$

explanations.text: The antecedent $p$ is an atom (Sahlqvist antecedent), and $\Diamond p$ is a positive formula — this is a Sahlqvist implication.
explanations.notes_quote: $p \to \Diamond p$: Sahlqvist (antecedent $p$ is an atom; $\Diamond p$ is positive)

explanations.text: $(p \wedge \Diamond\Box p) \to \Box p$ is the second worked example given in the lectures as a Sahlqvist formula.
explanations.notes_quote: $(p \wedge \Diamond\Box p) \to \Box p$: Sahlqvist (Example 2 from lectures)

explanations.text: $\Diamond\Box p \to \Box\Diamond p$ is NOT Sahlqvist — the notes explicitly state this, noting it also lacks a first-order correspondent.
explanations.notes_quote: $\Diamond\Box p \to \Box\Diamond p$: NOT Sahlqvist

explanations.text: $\Box p \to p$ has antecedent $\Box p$ (a boxed atom, which is a Sahlqvist antecedent) and consequent $p$ (a positive atom).
explanations.notes_quote: T (Reflexivity): $\Box p \to p$ | Reflexive | $\forall x\,R(x,x)$

explanations.text: $\Box p \to \Box\Box p$ has antecedent $\Box p$ (boxed atom) and consequent $\Box\Box p$ (which is positive). It is the Axiom 4 Sahlqvist formula.
explanations.notes_quote: 4 (Transitivity): $\Box p \to \Box\Box p$ | Transitive

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state Sahlqvist's Theorem (Theorem 11.8)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Every Sahlqvist formula has a first-order correspondent
There is an algorithm to compute the first-order correspondent of any Sahlqvist formula
Every modal formula has a first-order correspondent
Sahlqvist's Theorem provides a partial converse to Chagrova's Theorem by identifying a large computable class
Only Sahlqvist implications — not the full class of Sahlqvist formulas — have first-order correspondents

explanations.text: This is part (i) of Sahlqvist's Theorem.
explanations.notes_quote: Theorem 11.8 (Sahlqvist's Theorem): (i) Every Sahlqvist formula has a first-order correspondent

explanations.text: This is part (ii) of Sahlqvist's Theorem.
explanations.notes_quote: (ii) There is an algorithm to compute the first-order correspondent of any Sahlqvist formula

explanations.text: Only SAHLQVIST formulas are guaranteed first-order correspondents. Chagrova's Theorem shows the general question is undecidable.
Sahlqvist's Theorem identifies a large, practically useful subclass where the correspondence problem IS decidable (and computable).
explanations.notes_quote: However, large useful subclasses of formulas do have computable correspondents.

explanations.text: The theorem applies to the full class of Sahlqvist formulas (which includes more than just Sahlqvist implications, as they can also be closed under $\Box$ and variable-disjoint $\wedge$/$\vee$).

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe lambda expressions as used in the Sahlqvist–van Benthem algorithm? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $(\lambda x.F(x))$ denotes the unary predicate 'the set of all $x$ satisfying $F(x)$'
The evaluation rule is $(\lambda x.F(x))(t) = F(x/t)$, i.e., substitute $t$ for $x$ in $F(x)$
$(\lambda u.\, u = x)(y) = (y = x)$
$(\lambda u.\, u = x \vee R(y, u))(z) = (z = x \vee R(y, z))$
Lambda expressions can only denote binary predicates, not unary ones

explanations.text: Lambda expressions denote predicates/sets defined by a formula.
explanations.notes_quote: Given a formula $F(x)$ with free variable $x$, we write $(\lambda x.F(x))$ to denote the unary predicate 'the set of all those $x$ who satisfy $F(x)$'

explanations.text: Application of a lambda expression performs substitution.
explanations.notes_quote: Evaluation rule: Applying $(\lambda x.F(x))$ at term $t$ gives: $(\lambda x.F(x))(t) = F(x/t)$

explanations.text: Substituting $y$ for $u$ in $u = x$ gives $y = x$.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\lambda u.\ u = x)(y) = (y = x)$

explanations.text: Substituting $z$ for $u$ in $u = x \vee R(y, u)$ gives $z = x \vee R(y, z)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\lambda u.\ u = x \vee R(y, u))(z) = (z = x \vee R(y, z))$

explanations.text: Lambda expressions in this context denote UNARY predicates (sets of individuals satisfying a property), not binary predicates.

questions.text: Which of the following are correct statements about the relationship between lambda calculus and computation? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Lambda calculus is computationally equivalent to Turing Machines
Python's `lambda` functions implement the same concept
Lambda calculus is strictly weaker than Turing Machines
The $\lambda$-notation is used in the algorithm as a shorthand for describing predicates/subsets

explanations.text: Lambda calculus and Turing Machines are computationally equivalent.
explanations.notes_quote: Lambda expressions come from the Lambda calculus, which is computationally equivalent to Turing Machines.

explanations.text: Python's lambda keyword is a direct implementation of lambda calculus.
explanations.notes_quote: In Python, `lambda` functions are the same concept.

explanations.text: Lambda calculus is EQUIVALENT to Turing Machines in computational power, not strictly weaker.
In the algorithm, lambda expressions compactly describe the minimal valuation (predicate) for each propositional variable.
explanations.notes_quote: The algorithm uses $\lambda$-notation as a shorthand for describing predicates/subsets.

questions.text: What are the correct steps of the Sahlqvist–van Benthem algorithm? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Step 1: Compute the standard translation $\text{ST}_x(F)$
Step 2: Pull existential quantifiers from the antecedent to the front
Step 3: Group the antecedent into REL, AT, BOX, NEG $\to$ POS form
Step 4: Find the minimal valuation $\sigma(P_i)$ making AT $\wedge$ BOX trivially true
Step 5: Apply $\sigma$, substitute all occurrences of $P_i$, simplify, and universally quantify

explanations.text: Step 1 computes the standard translation.
explanations.notes_quote: Step 1: Compute the Standard Translation: Compute $\text{ST}_x(F)$.

explanations.text: Existentials in the antecedent must be pulled to the front using standard logical equivalences.
explanations.notes_quote: Step 2: Pull out 'Diamonds': Wherever an existential quantifier appears in the antecedent of $\text{ST}_x(F)$, pull it to the front

explanations.text: Step 3 organises the formula into its component parts.
explanations.notes_quote: Step 3: Group Terms: Identify a predicate symbol $P_i$ and reorganise the antecedent into the standard form: REL $\wedge$ AT $\wedge$ BOX $\wedge$ NEG $\to$ POS

explanations.text: The minimal valuation is the smallest set making AT and BOX simultaneously true.
explanations.notes_quote: Step 4: Read off Minimal Instances: Identify the minimum valuation $\sigma(P_i)$ that makes the conjunction (AT $\wedge$ BOX) true.

explanations.text: Step 5 applies the substitution and produces the first-order correspondent.
explanations.notes_quote: Step 5: Apply the Minimal Substitution: Apply $\sigma$ to $\text{ST}_x(F)$, substituting all occurrences of $P_i$ with $\sigma(P_i)$, and simplify.

questions.text: In Step 2 (Pull out Diamonds), which logical equivalences are used? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $(\exists x\,A(x)) \wedge B \equiv \exists x(A(x) \wedge B)$ when $x$ not free in $B$
$(\exists x\,A(x)) \to B \equiv \forall x(A(x) \to B)$ when $x$ not free in $B$
$(\forall x\,A(x)) \wedge B \equiv \forall x(A(x) \wedge B)$ when $x$ not free in $B$
These equivalences convert existentials in the antecedent into universal quantifiers at the front

explanations.text: This equivalence allows pulling an existential out of a conjunction.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\exists x\,A(x)) \wedge B \equiv \exists x(A(x) \wedge B)$ (when $x$ not free in $B$)

explanations.text: This equivalence converts an existential in the antecedent of an implication into a universal quantifier.
explanations.notes_quote: $(\exists x\,A(x)) \to B \equiv \forall x(A(x) \to B)$ (when $x$ not free in $B$)

explanations.text: This equivalence for universal quantifiers is not the one used in Step 2 — Step 2 specifically handles existential quantifiers (from $\Diamond$ translations).
The result of applying these equivalences is that existentials in the antecedent become universal quantifiers at the front of the formula.
explanations.notes_quote: This converts the formula to the form $\forall x_1 \ldots \forall x_n[\cdots]$ with all existentials promoted out.

questions.text: For $F = p \to \Diamond p$, which of the following are correct steps in the Sahlqvist–van Benthem algorithm? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\text{ST}_x(F) = P(x) \to \exists y(R(x,y) \wedge P(y))$
The minimal valuation is $\sigma(P) = \lambda u.\,(u = x)$
After applying $\sigma$, the result simplifies to $\exists y(R(x,y) \wedge y = x)$, i.e., $R(x,x)$
The first-order correspondent is $\forall x\,R(x,x)$ — reflexivity
The first-order correspondent is $\forall x\forall y(R(x,y) \to R(y,x))$ — symmetry

explanations.text: The standard translation applies $\text{ST}_x(p) = P(x)$ and $\text{ST}_x(\Diamond p) = \exists y(R(x,y) \wedge P(y))$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{ST}_x(F) = P(x) \to \exists y\bigl(R(x,y) \wedge P(y)\bigr)$

explanations.text: The only information about $P$ from AT is $P(x)$, so the minimal valuation assigns $P$ to be true exactly at $x$.
explanations.notes_quote: the minimal valuation assigns $P$ to be true at exactly $x$: $\sigma(P) = \lambda u.\ (u = x)$

explanations.text: Substituting $\sigma(P)$ for $P$ and simplifying yields $R(x,x)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $= \exists y\bigl(R(x,y) \wedge y = x\bigr) \equiv R(x,x)$

explanations.text: After universally quantifying over $x$, the first-order correspondent is $\forall x\,R(x,x)$ — reflexivity.
explanations.notes_quote: $\forall x\,\sigma(\text{ST}_x(F)) \equiv \forall x\,R(x,x)$. Conclusion: $p \to \Diamond p$ is valid precisely over the class of reflexive frames.

explanations.text: Symmetry $\forall x\forall y(R(x,y)\to R(y,x))$ is the correspondent of $p \to \Box\Diamond p$ (Axiom B), not $p \to \Diamond p$.

questions.text: For $F = (p \wedge \Diamond\Box p) \to \Box p$, which of the following are correct in the Sahlqvist–van Benthem analysis? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\text{ST}_x(F) = P(x) \wedge \exists y(R(x,y) \wedge \forall z(R(y,z) \to P(z))) \to \forall u(R(x,u) \to P(u))$
After pulling out the existential, the formula becomes universally quantified over $y$
The minimal valuation is $\sigma(P) = \lambda u.\,(u = x \vee R(y,u))$
AT = $P(x)$, REL = $R(x,y)$, BOX = $\forall z(R(y,z) \to P(z))$
The minimal valuation is $\sigma(P) = \lambda u.\,(u = x)$ only

explanations.text: This is the standard translation computed in Step 1 of Worked Example 2.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{ST}_x(F) = P(x) \wedge \exists y\bigl(R(x,y) \wedge \forall z(R(y,z) \to P(z))\bigr) \to \forall u\bigl(R(x,u) \to P(u)\bigr)$

explanations.text: Pulling $\exists y$ from the antecedent of an implication converts it to $\forall y$.
explanations.notes_quote: Using $(\exists y A(y)) \to B \equiv \forall y(A(y) \to B)$

explanations.text: $P$ must be true at $x$ (from AT) and at all $R$-successors of $y$ (from BOX), giving this minimal valuation.
explanations.notes_quote: $\sigma(P) = \lambda u.\ (u = x \vee R(y,u))$

explanations.text: These are the components as identified in Step 3 of the worked example.
explanations.notes_quote: $\underbrace{P(x)}_{\text{AT}} \wedge \underbrace{R(x,y)}_{\text{REL}} \wedge \underbrace{\forall z(R(y,z) \to P(z))}_{\text{BOX}} \to \underbrace{\forall u(R(x,u) \to P(u))}_{\text{POS}}$

explanations.text: $\sigma(P) = \lambda u.(u = x)$ would only account for AT. BOX forces $P$ also at all $R$-successors of $y$, so the full minimal valuation is $\lambda u.(u = x \vee R(y,u))$.

sections.title: 11.6 Standard Common Modal Axioms and Their Frame Correspondents

questions.text: Which of the following correctly match modal axioms to their frame correspondents? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Axiom T: $\Box p \to p$ — Reflexive frames: $\forall x\,R(x,x)$
Axiom 4: $\Box p \to \Box\Box p$ — Transitive frames: $\forall x\forall y\forall z(R(x,y)\wedge R(y,z)\to R(x,z))$
Axiom B: $p \to \Box\Diamond p$ — Symmetric frames: $\forall x\forall y(R(x,y)\to R(y,x))$
Axiom D: $\Box p \to \Diamond p$ — Serial frames: $\forall x\,\exists y\,R(x,y)$
Axiom K: $\Box(p\to q)\to(\Box p\to\Box q)$ — Valid in all frames (no frame restriction)

explanations.text: Axiom T corresponds to reflexivity.
explanations.notes_quote: T (Reflexivity): $\Box p \to p$ | Reflexive | $\forall x\,R(x,x)$

explanations.text: Axiom 4 corresponds to transitivity.
explanations.notes_quote: 4 (Transitivity): $\Box p \to \Box\Box p$ | Transitive | $\forall x\forall y\forall z(R(x,y)\wedge R(y,z)\to R(x,z))$

explanations.text: Axiom B corresponds to symmetry.
explanations.notes_quote: B (Symmetry): $p \to \Box\Diamond p$ | Symmetric | $\forall x\forall y(R(x,y)\to R(y,x))$

explanations.text: Axiom D corresponds to seriality (every world has a successor).
explanations.notes_quote: D: $\Box p \to \Diamond p$ | Serial | $\forall x\,\exists y\,R(x,y)$

explanations.text: Axiom K is valid in all frames — it imposes no frame restriction.
explanations.notes_quote: K: $\Box(p\to q)\to(\Box p\to\Box q)$ | All frames | $\top$ (always valid)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Axiom 5 and its frame correspondent? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Axiom 5: $\Diamond p \to \Box\Diamond p$
Axiom 5 corresponds to the Euclidean property: $\forall x\forall y\forall z(R(x,y)\wedge R(x,z)\to R(y,z))$
Axiom 5 corresponds to transitivity
The Euclidean property states: if $x$ can see $y$ and $z$, then $y$ can see $z$
Negative Introspection ($\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$) is related to Axiom 5

explanations.text: Axiom 5 is $\Diamond p \to \Box\Diamond p$.
explanations.notes_quote: 5: $\Diamond p \to \Box\Diamond p$ | Euclidean

explanations.text: Axiom 5 corresponds to the Euclidean frame property.
explanations.notes_quote: 5: $\Diamond p \to \Box\Diamond p$ | Euclidean | $\forall x\forall y\forall z(R(x,y)\wedge R(x,z)\to R(y,z))$

explanations.text: Transitivity corresponds to Axiom 4 ($\Box p \to \Box\Box p$), not Axiom 5.
This is the informal description of the Euclidean property.
explanations.notes_quote: Euclidean property (if $x$ can see $y$ and $z$, then $y$ can see $z$)

explanations.text: Negative Introspection is equivalent to Axiom 5 ($\Diamond p \to \Box\Diamond p$) by duality of $\Box$ and $\Diamond$.
explanations.notes_quote: R2 ($\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$): This is the 5 axiom. Equivalent to $\Diamond p \to \Box\Diamond p$ (by duality).

questions.text: Which of the following correctly give the standard translation of $F = p \to \Box\Diamond p$? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\text{ST}_x(F) = P(x) \to \forall y\bigl(R(x,y) \to \exists z(R(y,z) \wedge P(z))\bigr)$
$\text{ST}_x(F) = P(x) \to \exists y(R(x,y) \wedge \forall z(R(y,z) \to P(z)))$
This is the standard translation of Axiom B ($p \to \Box\Diamond p$)
This formula corresponds to the symmetric frame property
$\text{ST}_x(F) = \forall y(R(x,y)\to P(y)) \to P(x)$

explanations.text: This is the correct standard translation: $\Box$ becomes $\forall y(R(x,y) \to \cdots)$ and $\Diamond$ becomes $\exists z(R(y,z) \wedge \cdots)$.
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{ST}_x(F) = P(x) \to \forall y\bigl(R(x,y) \to \exists z(R(y,z) \wedge P(z))\bigr)$

explanations.text: Swapping $\forall/\exists$ gives the translation of $p \to \Diamond\Box p$, not $p \to \Box\Diamond p$.
Axiom B is $p \to \Box\Diamond p$.
explanations.notes_quote: B (Symmetry): $p \to \Box\Diamond p$ | Symmetric | $\forall x\forall y(R(x,y)\to R(y,x))$

explanations.text: Axiom B corresponds to the symmetric frame property.
explanations.notes_quote: B (Symmetry): $p \to \Box\Diamond p$ | Symmetric | $\forall x\forall y(R(x,y)\to R(y,x))$

explanations.text: This is the standard translation of $\Box p \to p$ (Axiom T), not $p \to \Box\Diamond p$.

questions.source_ref: Section 11.7: SGT 11.2 — Full Sahlqvist–van Benthem Worked Example
questions.text: For $F = \Diamond(p \wedge \Diamond\Box p) \to \Box p$, which of the following are correct in the Sahlqvist–van Benthem analysis? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Pulling out both existentials converts the formula to a $\forall y_1 \forall y_2$ universal prefix
REL = $R(x,y_1) \wedge R(y_1,y_2)$, AT = $P(y_1)$, BOX = $\forall z(R(y_2,z)\to P(z))$
The minimal valuation is $\sigma(P) = \lambda u.\,(u = y_1 \vee R(y_2, u))$
The first-order correspondent is $\forall x\forall y_1\forall y_2[R(x,y_1)\wedge R(y_1,y_2) \to \forall u(R(x,u)\to(u=y_1\vee R(y_2,u)))]$
The formula is not a Sahlqvist formula and so the algorithm cannot be applied

explanations.text: Two rounds of pulling existentials (for $\exists y_1$ and $\exists y_2$) create a $\forall y_1\forall y_2$ prefix.
explanations.notes_quote: $\equiv \forall y_1\forall y_2\Bigl[R(x,y_1) \wedge P(y_1) \wedge R(y_1,y_2) \wedge \forall z(R(y_2,z)\to P(z)) \to \forall u(R(x,u)\to P(u))\Bigr]$

explanations.text: These are the components as identified in Step 3.
explanations.notes_quote: $\underbrace{R(x,y_1) \wedge R(y_1,y_2)}_{\text{REL}} \wedge \underbrace{P(y_1)}_{\text{AT}} \wedge \underbrace{\forall z(R(y_2,z)\to P(z))}_{\text{BOX}}$

explanations.text: $P$ must be true at $y_1$ (AT) and at all $R$-successors of $y_2$ (BOX).
explanations.notes_quote: $\sigma(P) = \lambda u.\ (u = y_1 \vee R(y_2, u))$

explanations.text: This is the final first-order correspondent after applying $\sigma$ and universally quantifying.
explanations.notes_quote: $\forall x\forall y_1\forall y_2\Bigl[R(x,y_1)\wedge R(y_1,y_2) \to \forall u\bigl(R(x,u)\to(u=y_1\vee R(y_2,u))\bigr)\Bigr]$

explanations.text: $\Diamond(p \wedge \Diamond\Box p) \to \Box p$ IS a Sahlqvist formula — the antecedent is built from atoms and boxed atoms under $\Diamond$, and the consequent $\Box p$ is positive.

questions.text: In SGT 11.3, Raymond models agent beliefs with $\Box F =$ 'the agent believes $F$'. Which of the following correctly match criteria to modal formulas and frame properties? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: R1 ($\Box p \to \Box\Box p$) — Axiom 4 — Transitive frames
R2 ($\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$) — Axiom 5 — Euclidean frames
R3 (consistency of beliefs) — related to serial frames: $\forall x\exists y\,R(x,y)$
R4 ($\Box p \wedge \Box(p\to q) \to \Box q$) — Axiom K — Valid in all frames
R4 corresponds to reflexive frames

explanations.text: R1 is Positive Introspection / Axiom 4, corresponding to transitivity.
explanations.notes_quote: R1 ($\Box p \to \Box\Box p$): This is the 4 axiom... First-order correspondent: $\forall x\forall y\forall z(R(x,y)\wedge R(y,z)\to R(x,z))$ — transitivity.

explanations.text: R2 is Negative Introspection / Axiom 5, corresponding to the Euclidean property.
explanations.notes_quote: R2 ($\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$): This is the 5 axiom... First-order correspondent: $\forall x\forall y\forall z(R(x,y)\wedge R(x,z)\to R(y,z))$ — Euclidean property.

explanations.text: R3 (consistency) corresponds to serial frames — every world has a successor.
explanations.notes_quote: R3: The formula $\Box\bot \to \bot$ corresponds to serial frames: $\forall x\exists y\,R(x,y)$

explanations.text: R4 is equivalent to Axiom K, which is valid in all frames with no restriction.
explanations.notes_quote: R4: Equivalent to $\Box(p\to q)\to(\Box p\to\Box q)$ — this is the K axiom, valid in all frames.

explanations.text: R4 corresponds to ALL frames (Axiom K), not reflexive frames. Reflexivity corresponds to Axiom T ($\Box p \to p$).

questions.text: Regarding the tricky criterion R* in SGT 11.3 ($\Box(\Box p \to p) \to \Box p$), which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: R* is not a Sahlqvist formula because $\Box p$ appears with mixed polarity (both positive and negative)
The Sahlqvist–van Benthem algorithm cannot be applied to R*
By Chagrova's Theorem, it is undecidable in general whether R* has a first-order correspondent
R* is a Sahlqvist formula and the algorithm produces $\forall x\,R(x,x)$
The antecedent $\Box(\Box p \to p)$ contains $\Box p$ in a negative position, violating Sahlqvist antecedent conditions

explanations.text: $\Box p$ appears negatively in the antecedent and positively in the consequent — mixed polarity prevents minimal valuation identification.
explanations.notes_quote: This formula has $\Box p$ appearing both positively and negatively — it is not a Sahlqvist formula.

explanations.text: Without a minimal substitution (blocked by mixed polarity), the algorithm cannot proceed.
explanations.notes_quote: Therefore the Sahlqvist–van Benthem algorithm cannot be applied

explanations.text: For non-Sahlqvist formulas, the general undecidability of frame correspondence (Chagrova's Theorem) applies.
explanations.notes_quote: it is unclear (undecidable in general by Chagrova's Theorem) whether this formula even has a first-order frame correspondent

explanations.text: R* is NOT a Sahlqvist formula and the algorithm cannot be applied to it.
In $\Box(\Box p \to p) = \Box(\neg\Box p \vee p)$, the $\Box p$ inside has negative polarity — this violates Sahlqvist antecedent conditions.
explanations.notes_quote: the antecedent $\Box(\Box p \to p)$ contains $\Box p$ in a negative position (inside $\Box p \to p$, which is $\neg\Box p \vee p$, so $\Box p$ has negative polarity)

questions.text: Which of the following correctly match terms to their definitions from the Key Definitions table? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Epistemic logic: $\Box p$ means 'the agent knows $p$'
Deontic logic: $\Box p$ means 'the agent believes $p$'
Standard translation $\text{ST}_x(F)$: inductive translation of modal formula $F$ into first-order logic
Chagrova's Theorem: it is undecidable whether a modal axiom has a first-order correspondent
Minimal valuation $\sigma(P_i)$: the smallest valuation making AT $\wedge$ BOX trivially true

explanations.text: Epistemic logic uses $\Box$ for knowledge.
explanations.notes_quote: Epistemic logic: Modal logic where $\Box p$ means 'the agent knows $p$'

explanations.text: Deontic logic interprets $\Box p$ as '$p$ is obligatory', not 'the agent believes $p$'. Belief is doxastic logic.
The standard translation maps modal formulas to first-order predicate logic.
explanations.notes_quote: Standard translation $\text{ST}_x(F)$: Inductive translation of modal formula $F$ into first-order predicate logic relative to variable $x$

explanations.text: This is the exact statement of Chagrova's Theorem as listed in the Key Definitions.
explanations.notes_quote: Chagrova's Theorem: It is undecidable whether a given modal axiom has a first-order correspondent

explanations.text: The minimal valuation finds the fewest worlds making AT and BOX trivially satisfied.
explanations.notes_quote: Minimal valuation $\sigma(P_i)$: The smallest (fewest worlds) valuation making AT $\wedge$ BOX trivially true

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the True Knowledge, Positive Introspection, and Negative Introspection axioms and their frame correspondents? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: True Knowledge $\Box p \to p$ is valid on reflexive frames
Positive Introspection $\Box p \to \Box\Box p$ is valid on transitive frames
Negative Introspection $\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$ is valid on Euclidean frames
True Knowledge $\Box p \to p$ is valid on transitive frames
Negative Introspection is equivalent to Axiom 5 ($\Diamond p \to \Box\Diamond p$) by duality

explanations.text: True Knowledge / Axiom T corresponds to reflexivity.
explanations.notes_quote: True Knowledge axiom: $\Box p \to p$ — valid on reflexive frames

explanations.text: Positive Introspection / Axiom 4 corresponds to transitivity.
explanations.notes_quote: Positive Introspection axiom: $\Box p \to \Box\Box p$ — valid on transitive frames

explanations.text: Negative Introspection / Axiom 5 corresponds to the Euclidean property.
explanations.notes_quote: Negative Introspection axiom: $\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$ — valid on Euclidean frames

explanations.text: True Knowledge corresponds to REFLEXIVE frames, not transitive frames. Transitivity corresponds to Positive Introspection.
By duality of $\Box$ and $\Diamond$, $\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$ is equivalent to $\Diamond p \to \Box\Diamond p$ (Axiom 5).
explanations.notes_quote: R2 ($\neg\Box p \to \Box\neg\Box p$): This is the 5 axiom. Equivalent to $\Diamond p \to \Box\Diamond p$ (by duality).

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Sahlqvist–van Benthem algorithm components? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: REL is a conjunction of relational formulas of the form $R(x,y)$
AT is a conjunction of translations of atoms: formulas of the form $P_i(x)$
BOX is a conjunction of translations of boxed atoms
POS is a positive formula (the consequent of the Sahlqvist implication)
REL in the grouped form refers to modal formulas, not relational first-order atoms

explanations.text: REL contains first-order atoms describing accessibility relations.
explanations.notes_quote: REL is a conjunction of relational formulas of the form $R(x,y)$ for variables $x,y$

explanations.text: AT contains the first-order translations of propositional variable atoms.
explanations.notes_quote: AT is a conjunction of (translations of) atoms of the form $P_i(x)$ for variable $x$

explanations.text: BOX contains the first-order translations of boxed atoms (universal quantifications).
explanations.notes_quote: BOX is a conjunction of (translations of) boxed atoms of the form $\text{ST}_x(\Box^n p_i)$

explanations.text: POS is the positive consequent of the Sahlqvist implication.
explanations.notes_quote: POS is a positive formula with respect to $P_i$

explanations.text: REL contains first-order RELATIONAL formulas $R(x,y)$ — they are first-order atomic formulas, not modal formulas.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly summarise the key learning objectives of Week 11? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Different interpretations of $\Box$ (knowledge, belief, obligation, time) require restricting to appropriate frame classes
$\text{ST}_x(F)$ translates a modal formula into first-order logic, mapping $\Diamond$ to $\exists$ and $\Box$ to $\forall$
Frame validity requires quantifying over predicates, taking us into second-order logic
Every modal formula has a computable first-order frame correspondent
The Sahlqvist–van Benthem algorithm has five steps: ST computation, pull diamonds, group terms, minimal valuation, substitution and simplification

explanations.text: The first learning objective emphasises the need for frame classes.
explanations.notes_quote: LO 1 (Frame classes and applications): Different interpretations of $\Box$ require different frame properties. Restricting to appropriate classes of frames validates the desired tautologies.

explanations.text: The standard translation maps modal operators to first-order quantifiers.
explanations.notes_quote: LO 2 (Standard translation): $\text{ST}_x(F)$ translates a modal formula into first-order predicate logic, mapping $\Diamond$ to $\exists$ and $\Box$ to $\forall$.

explanations.text: Predicate quantification is needed for frame validity, which is second-order.
explanations.notes_quote: LO 3 (Second-order problem): Frame validity requires quantifying over predicates $P_1,\ldots,P_n$, taking us into second-order logic.

explanations.text: Chagrova's Theorem shows that it is UNDECIDABLE whether a modal formula has a first-order correspondent — not every formula does.
The five steps are correctly summarised in LO 8.
explanations.notes_quote: LO 8 (The algorithm): Five steps: (1) compute $\text{ST}_x(F)$; (2) pull existentials to front; (3) group into REL/AT/BOX/NEG $\to$ POS; (4) find minimal valuation $\sigma(P_i)$; (5) apply $\sigma$, simplify, and universally quantify.
